alleged_violations_and_damages.specific_portions_violated: Alleged violations include, but may not be limited to: Section 4b(a)(2)(A)-(C) of the Commodity Exchange Act (7 U.S.C. § 6b) regarding fraud by commodity brokers; Section 4o(1) of the Commodity Exchange Act (7 U.S.C. § 6o(1)) regarding fraudulent conduct by commodity trading advisors; and CFTC Regulation 166.3 (17 C.F.R. § 166.3) regarding failure to diligently supervise the handling of customer accounts.
alleged_violations_and_damages.description_of_complaint:
I, Jonathan Michael Harris, am a 47-year-old small business owner residing at 1842 West Addison Street, Apartment 3B, Chicago, Illinois. I operate a specialty printing business on the North Side of Chicago and have no professional background in commodities trading. Prior to opening the account described below, my investment experience was limited to mutual funds and a 401(k) plan at my previous employer.

In April 2023, I was contacted by Respondent Lakefront Futures & Commodities, LLC ("Lakefront"), through its associated person, Respondent Mark A. Donovan ("Donovan"). Donovan obtained my contact information after I attended a free online webinar about inflation and commodity markets hosted by Lakefront on or about 2023-04-10. Following the webinar, Donovan called me at my cell phone (which is also my home number listed on this form) on or about 2023-04-12 and began aggressively soliciting me to open a managed futures trading account.

During multiple phone calls between 2023-04-12 and 2023-04-25, Donovan represented to me that he had over 15 years of experience trading crude oil and agricultural futures, that his managed accounts program had "never had a losing year," and that he could reasonably target 15–20% annual returns with "limited downside" by using stop-loss orders. He repeatedly emphasized that futures were an effective hedge against inflation and that, by allocating a portion of my savings to his program, I would be protecting my purchasing power while giving myself the opportunity for additional growth.

I repeatedly told Donovan that I was risk-averse, that the money I was considering investing represented a large portion of my personal savings (proceeds from a home sale and an inheritance from my late father), and that I could not afford to lose it. I specifically recall a call on 2023-04-18 where I stated, in substance, "Mark, I am not a gambler. I cannot afford to lose 30 or 40 percent of this money." Donovan responded along the lines of, "Jonathan, I understand completely. With the way we structure the trades and the stop orders, we are not going to let anything like that happen. The risk is controlled."

On or about 2023-04-26, at Donovan's urging, I completed Lakefront's new account documents electronically. In those documents, my investment objective was marked as "speculation" and my risk tolerance as "high," even though I had stated verbally that I was conservative and primarily interested in capital preservation. I did not notice these discrepancies at the time because Donovan rushed me through the electronic signature process over the phone, instructing me to "just click through" as he said it was all "standard boilerplate." I later obtained copies of these forms in September 2023 and saw that they did not accurately reflect what I had told Donovan.

On 2023-05-02, I wired $150,000 to Lakefront to fund the managed futures account. Donovan told me this was an appropriate starting amount for his trading program and that he would be personally handling the trades with my "input and approval." In reality, after the account was opened, Donovan engaged in frequent, highly leveraged trading in crude oil, natural gas, and corn futures and options without obtaining my specific prior approval for each trade.

From May through August 2023, the account statements (which I accessed online) showed a pattern of large, rapid trades, often opened and closed within a day or two. On at least three occasions (around 2023-06-09, 2023-07-14, and 2023-08-03), I called Donovan to express concern about the volatility and the growing losses. Each time, he told me that the drawdowns were "temporary," that we were "building positions" ahead of expected market moves, and that pulling back at that point would "lock in losses" and "miss the recovery." He also told me on 2023-07-14 that if I added another $50,000, he could "average down" and "get the account back on track" more quickly.

Based on those assurances, on 2023-07-17 I wired an additional $50,000 to the account, bringing my total deposits to $200,000. Donovan again reiterated that the downside was "limited" and that he had "a specific strategy" that had worked for many clients in the past. At no point did he clearly explain the magnitude of the leverage being used, the potential for margin calls, or the realistic range of potential losses.

In late August and early September 2023, the energy markets moved sharply against the positions in my account. On or about 2023-09-01, I received an automated email from Lakefront indicating a margin deficiency. This was the first time I became aware that the account was on margin. I immediately called Donovan, who told me not to worry and that he would "handle it." However, over the next several trading days, the account suffered rapid and severe losses.

When I finally insisted on a full explanation in a call on 2023-09-08, Donovan admitted that the account had lost approximately 65% of its value and that my equity balance was around $70,000. I was shocked and devastated. I told him I wanted to stop all trading and close the account. He asked me to "give it a couple more weeks" to see if the market would turn. I refused and submitted a written instruction via email on 2023-09-09 directing Lakefront to cease all trading and liquidate the positions.

By 2023-09-12, the remaining open positions were closed. The final account value, after commissions and fees, was $68,423.17. Over the course of approximately four and a half months, I had lost $131,576.83 of the $200,000 I had deposited. A review of the account statements shows substantial commissions and fees charged on each trade, frequent in-and-out trading that appears designed to generate commissions, and position sizes that I now understand were inconsistent with any reasonable risk management for a conservative client.

I believe that Lakefront and Donovan engaged in fraudulent misrepresentations and omissions in soliciting my account and in managing it thereafter. Specifically:

1. They misrepresented the risk of the trading program by emphasizing potential returns and "limited downside" while failing to adequately disclose the possibility of large, rapid losses due to leverage and margin.
2. They mischaracterized my risk tolerance and investment objectives on the new account forms, marking me as a high-risk speculator despite my clear oral statements to the contrary.
3. Donovan executed trades in my account on a discretionary basis without obtaining my specific prior approval for each trade, even though I believed I would be consulted and would have the opportunity to approve or reject trades.
4. They failed to properly supervise the trading activity in my account, allowing excessive trading volume and large position sizes that were unsuitable given my financial situation and expressed objectives.
5. They placed their own interest in generating commissions ahead of my interest in preserving capital, as evidenced by the high number of trades and the substantial portion of my losses attributable to fees and commissions.

As a result of this conduct, I have suffered significant financial harm. The $131,576.83 lost represented funds I had earmarked for my children's college education and for a down payment on a future home. I have two children, ages 14 and 11, and this loss has forced me to reconsider their college plans and has caused substantial emotional stress within my family. I have also had to delay planned investments in my printing business and take on additional hours and side work to try to rebuild our savings.

I am filing this reparations complaint because I believe that Lakefront Futures & Commodities, LLC and Mark A. Donovan violated the Commodity Exchange Act and CFTC regulations by making material misrepresentations, omitting material facts, engaging in unauthorized and unsuitable trading, and failing to supervise the handling of my account. I respectfully request that the Commission award me reparations for my losses, as detailed below.
alleged_violations_and_damages.explanation_of_how_damages_were_calculat:
The total damages claimed represent the net out-of-pocket trading losses I incurred in my managed futures account at Lakefront Futures & Commodities, LLC, excluding any interest or consequential damages, calculated as follows:

1. Total funds deposited into the account:
   - Initial wire on 2023-05-02: $150,000.00
   - Additional wire on 2023-07-17: $50,000.00
   - Total deposits: $200,000.00

2. Final account value after liquidation of all positions and deduction of commissions and fees:
   - Account equity as of 2023-09-12 (per closing statement): $68,423.17

3. Net trading loss:
   - Total deposits: $200,000.00
   - Less final account value: $68,423.17
   - Net loss: $131,576.83

This $131,576.83 figure is taken directly from a spreadsheet I created by reconciling each monthly statement from May through September 2023 with my bank records. I verified the deposit amounts using my bank's wire transfer confirmations and matched them to the credits shown on the Lakefront account statements. I then used the final September 2023 statement to confirm the closing equity value of $68,423.17 after all trades were closed and all commissions and fees were deducted.

I am not, at this time, including a separate claim for lost opportunity costs, emotional distress, or the additional income tax consequences of liquidating other investments to cover expenses that this loss made harder to meet. I am also not including any interest that might otherwise be calculable from the date of loss. My request is limited to the straightforward out-of-pocket loss amount of $131,576.83 described above.

If necessary, I can provide the Commission with copies of:
- All monthly and daily account statements from Lakefront for the period 2023-05-01 through 2023-09-30;
- Wire transfer confirmations from my bank (First Chicago Community Bank) showing the $150,000 and $50,000 deposits; and
- A summary spreadsheet detailing the running account balance, deposits, withdrawals (none, in this case), and the final equity value used to compute the net loss.

Accordingly, the amount of reparations I am seeking in this complaint is $131,576.83, representing my net out-of-pocket losses attributable to the alleged misconduct of the respondents.
